# Terminal Music Player Comparison

A survey of terminal-based music players for Linux, focused on players relevant to the
Omarchy desktop environment and the terminal-music space more broadly.

This document compares **must**, **cliamp**, **cmus**, **ncmpcpp + MPD**, **rmpc**,
**kew**, and **spotify-tui + spotifyd** across architecture, features, desktop
integration, and design philosophy.

**Note**: Other than this note, this document was entirely generated by an LLM.  While I find
the analysis reasonable, and have at least lightly used all of the options discussed (except spotify-tui),
I would not at all be surprised to learn some of what is noted may be in error.  Please advise and I
will be happy to make any needed corrections and/or improvements.

---

## TL;DR

| Player | Best for | Notable limitation |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **must** | Album art in TUI + local/Subsonic hybrid + discoverable keybindings | mpv dependency, no EQ, fewer streaming sources |
| **cliamp** | Breadth of streaming providers + EQ + plugin system + Quickshell widget | No album art, 100+ keybindings to learn, heaviest binary |
| **cmus** | Battle-tested simplicity, fastest startup, zero config | No album art, no visualizer, no streaming |
| **ncmpcpp+MPD** | Maximum flexibility, modular setup, largest ecosystem | Requires learning two programs (server + client) |
| **rmpc** | Modern MPD client with album art + visualizer | Requires MPD, less mature than ncmpcpp |
| **kew** | CLI-first "just type what you want" + best Sixel album art + visualizer | No streaming at all, no plugin system |
| **spotify-tui+spotifyd** | Deep Spotify ecosystem, 19k stars | Premium required, no album art, no local files |

---

## The Players

### must

*Private project — Go, MIT*

A fast TUI music player for local music libraries and Subsonic-compatible streaming
servers (Navidrome, Jellyfin, etc.). Built on Bubble Tea v2 (Go TUI framework).
Can play local files and Subsonic streams in the same playlist simultaneously.
Useable as a local-only player, a Subsonic client only, or both at once.

**Key features:** Prominent high-res album art (Kitty, iTerm2, Sixel, halfblocks),
artist bios/discographies/galleries, temp/download directory browser (staging area
for tracks you haven't committed to your library yet), 9 real-time audio visualizers
(adapted from [cliamp](https://github.com/bjarneo/cliamp)), lyrics from LRCLib,
FTS5 full-text search, 4 layout modes, session restore, sleep timer & alarm,
ReplayGain, Last.fm + ListenBrainz scrobbling, Lidarr integration.

**Ecosystem:** must has a sibling project — [rptui](https://github.com/pdfrg/rptui),
a Radio Paradise TUI. Both share layouts, keybindings, visualizers, themes, Omarchy
integration, and codebase DNA. Together they cover personal music library + Subsonic
streaming (must) and human-curated internet radio (rptui).

**CLI-first play:** `must play <query>` (or `must p <query>`) works whether or not
must is already running. If no instance is running, it auto-starts the TUI, searches
the library, and begins playback — with smart resolution (artist → all albums in
order, track → full album from that position). `must ps <query>` enables shuffle.
This gives must a kew-like "just type what you want" flow while keeping its richer
TUI and feature set.

**Omarchy integration:** Native — reads `~/.config/omarchy/current/theme/colors.toml`
and live-reloads when the desktop theme changes. All screenshots use the Omarchy
Vurple theme.

### cliamp

*[github.com/bjarneo/cliamp](https://github.com/bjarneo/cliamp) — Go, MIT — ~2,600 stars*

A terminal music player inspired by Winamp. The most feature-rich terminal player
by source count, supporting 15+ streaming backends, a 10-band parametric EQ,
built-in and Lua-plugin visualizers, and a Quickshell desktop widget.

**Key features:** Native Spotify (go-librespot, Premium required), YouTube
(via yt-dlp), SoundCloud, Bandcamp, Bilibili, NetEase, Plex, Jellyfin, Emby,
Navidrome, 30k+ radio stations, podcasts. 10-band parametric EQ with presets.
Lua plugin system (sandboxed). 20 built-in themes. Headless daemon mode.
MPRIS support.

**Omarchy integration:** Two tiers — (1) official Quickshell now-playing widget
in `contrib/quickshell/` with live spectrum data, Omarchy color reading, and Winamp-
style segmented visualizer. (2) Community `cliamp-omarchy-theme` hook that
regenerates cliamp themes on Omarchy theme switches.

### cmus

*[cmus.github.io](https://cmus.github.io) — C, GPL-2.0 — ~6,000 stars*

The most established all-in-one terminal music player (first release ~2004).
A single binary that handles indexing, database, and playback with no server
dependency. Instant startup even with huge libraries.

**Key features:** Extremely fast. Gapless playback, ReplayGain, SHOUTcast
streaming. Vi-style keybindings with fully remappable commands and `:command`
mode with tab completion. MPRIS built-in (since v2.8). UNIX socket remote
control. CUE sheet support. Live filtering (type to filter).

**Omarchy integration:** None. Uses terminal emulator colors. MPRIS works for
media keys. No theme reading or widget.

### ncmpcpp + MPD

*[mpd: musicpd.org](https://www.musicpd.org) — C++, GPL-2.0 — ~2,200 stars*
*[ncmpcpp: github.com/ncmpcpp/ncmpcpp](https://github.com/ncmpcpp/ncmpcpp) — C++, GPL-2.0 — ~2,400 stars*

The classic modular setup: MPD is a headless daemon handling audio, database,
and playlists. ncmpcpp is the most popular TUI client. You can also use any
other MPD client (there are dozens).

**Key features:** MPD plays virtually any format. ncmpcpp adds a tag editor,
search engine with regex, music visualizer (via FIFO + FFTW, requires config),
Last.fm artist info, filesystem browser, highly customizable song format,
and fully remappable keybindings. MPRIS via `mpd-mpris` bridge.

**Omarchy integration:** None. MPD has its own colors. MPRIS bridge works for
media keys but no theme awareness.

### rmpc

*[github.com/mierak/rmpc](https://github.com/mierak/rmpc) — Rust, GPL-3.0 — ~3,100 stars*

A modern MPD client with excellent terminal image support. Combines a
ranger/lf-style three-column browser with album art (Kitty, Sixel, iTerm2,
Ueberzug++), Cava visualizer integration, LRC synced lyrics, and YouTube
playback.

**Key features:** Terminal album art via multiple protocols. Cava-based
music visualization. LRC lyrics. Vim-like keybindings (fully remappable).
Ranger-style column browser. Script hooks and CLI mode. Highly configurable
UI (header, columns, queue). Requires MPD running.

**Omarchy integration:** None. Same as ncmpcpp — terminal-dependent colors,
MPRIS via `mpd-mpris` bridge.

### kew

*[codeberg.org/ravachol/kew](https://codeberg.org/ravachol/kew) — C, GPL-2.0 — ~2,800 stars*

"Music for the Shell" — a rapidly growing terminal player with a unique CLI-first
interaction model. Instead of browsing a file tree, you type `kew nirvana` and
it plays matching content. Excellent album art via Sixel.

**Key features:** Full-color Sixel album art via Chafa (ASCII fallback). Built-in
spectrum visualizer + external Chroma integration. LRC and embedded tag lyrics.
CLI-first: `kew <keyword>` generates smart playlists from search terms.
26+ built-in themes. Album-color-derived themes (picks 4 colors from the
current cover). Gapless + experimental crossfade. Desktop notifications,
Discord RPC, MPRIS. Completely offline by design (no streaming).

**Omarchy integration:** None. Has compatible themes (Catppuccin, Gruvbox,
Nord, Kanagawa, Tokyo Night) but no Omarchy theme reading.

### spotify-tui + spotifyd

*[spt: github.com/Rigellute/spotify-tui](https://github.com/Rigellute/spotify-tui) — Rust, MIT — ~19,200 stars*
*[spotifyd: github.com/spotifyd/spotifyd](https://github.com/spotifyd/spotifyd) — Rust, GPL-3.0 — ~10,700 stars*

A two-part setup: spotifyd is a lightweight Spotify Connect daemon that streams
audio. spotify-tui (spt) is a TUI client to browse, search, and control
playback. Together they provide a keyboard-driven Spotify experience without
the official desktop client.

**Key features:** Browse library, playlists, albums, artists. Search tracks,
albums, playlists. Device switching. CLI mode for scripting. Fully configurable
theme with RGB colors. Configurable keybindings. Requires Spotify Premium.

**Omarchy integration:** None. spotifyd supports MPRIS.

---

## Feature Comparison

### Playback

| Feature | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|---------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Audio backend | mpv (external) | Built-in (miniaudio/ffmpeg) | Built-in (plugins) | MPD (external) | MPD (external) | Built-in (miniaudio) | spotifyd |
| Gapless | Yes | Yes | Yes | Via MPD | Via MPD | Yes | Via Spotify |
| ReplayGain | Yes | No | Yes | Via MPD | Via MPD | Yes | No |
| EQ | No | 10-band parametric | No | No | No | No | No |
| Speed control | No | Yes (] / [) | No | No | No | No | No |
| Crossfade | No | No | No | Via MPD | No | Experimental | No |

### Sources

| Source | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|--------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Local files | Yes | Yes | Yes | Via MPD | Via MPD | Yes | No |
| Subsonic/Navidrome | **Yes** (native) | Yes (native) | No | Via MPD plugin | No | No | No |
| Spotify | No | **Yes** (native, Premium) | No | No | No | No | **Yes** (Premium) |
| YouTube | No | **Yes** (yt-dlp) | No | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| SoundCloud | No | **Yes** (yt-dlp) | No | No | No | No | No |
| Plex/Jellyfin/Emby | No | **Yes** (native) | No | No | No | No | No |
| Radio | No | **Yes** (30k+ stations) | SHOUTcast | No | No | No | No |
| Podcasts | No | **Yes** (RSS) | No | Via MPD | No | No | No |
| Bandcamp/Bilibili/NetEase | No | **Yes** (yt-dlp) | No | No | No | No | No |

### Display

| Feature | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|---------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Album art (terminal) | **Full-width, prominent** — Kitty, iTerm2, Sixel, halfblocks | Metadata headers only | No | No | Kitty, Sixel, iTerm2, Ueberzug++ | **Sixel + ASCII** via Chafa | No |
| Visualizers | **9 built-in** (adapted from cliamp) | Built-in + **Lua plugin** visualizers | No | Via FIFO + FFTW | Via Cava (external) | Built-in spectrum + Chroma | No |
| Lyrics | **Synced + plain** (LRCLib) | Synced (Navidrome, local) | No | Via plugin | **LRC synced** | LRC, Vorbis, SYLT, USLT | No |
| Artist info | **Bio, discography, gallery** | No | No | Last.fm fetch | No | No | No |
| Album art size | **Dominant panel** | None (headers only) | N/A | N/A | Side panel | Side / toggle | N/A |

### Integrations & Services

| Feature | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|---------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Scrobbling | **Last.fm + ListenBrainz** | Via Lua plugin | Via cmusfm | Built-in (Last.fm) | No | No | No |
| Lidarr | **Yes** | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| Temp directory browser | **Yes** (staging for pre-library tracks) | No | No | No | No | No | No |

### UI & Control

| Feature | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|---------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Keybinding style | **Footer always visible** — no memorization | Comprehensive reference needed | Vi-style + `:command` mode | Vi-style, fully remappable | Vim-like, remappable | Single-letter + F-keys | Configurable |
| Keybinding remap | No | No | **Yes** (fully) | **Yes** (fully) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Layout options | 4 (large/medium/compact/narrow) | Compact mode | Fixed | Extended song format | Configurable columns | **Custom layouts** (4.1+) | Fixed |
| Browser style | 3-column (artists > albums > tracks) | Split pane + provider browser | Tree / flat / filter | Library / browser / playlist | **Ranger-style** 3-column | CLI-first + F3 explorer | Spotify-style |
| Search | FTS5 full-text, field queries | Fuzzy search in playlists | Live filter + `/` | Regex search | Fuzzy | Keyword -> smart playlist | Search API |
| IPC / remote | **Unix socket** — `must play` etc. | Unix socket — `cliamp next` | Unix socket — `cmus-remote` | MPD protocol (network) | MPD protocol | `--noui` mode | Spotify Connect |
| MPRIS | Via mpv-mpris | **Built-in** | **Built-in** | Via mpd-mpris | Via mpd-mpris | **Built-in** | Via spotifyd |
| Headless/daemon | No | **Yes** (`--daemon`) | No | **MPD is daemon** | No | `--noui` | **spotifyd is daemon** |
| Themes (built-in) | 6 | 20 | Limited colors | Extensive colors | No | **26+** | RGB config |
| Omarchy theme live-reload | **Native** | Via community hook | No | No | No | No | No |

### Project

| Metric | must | cliamp | cmus | ncmpcpp+MPD | rmpc | kew | spotify-tui+d |
|--------|------|--------|------|-------------|------|-----|---------------|
| Language | Go | Go | C | C++ + C | Rust | C | Rust + Rust |
| License | MIT | MIT | GPL-2.0 | GPL-2.0 | GPL-3.0 | GPL-2.0 | MIT + GPL-3.0 |
| Age | ~2026 | ~2024 | ~2004 | ~2006 + ~2007 | ~2023 | ~2023 | ~2019 + ~2018 |
| Activity | Active | Very active | Active | Maintenance | Active | **Very active** | Active |
| Plugin system | No | **Lua (sandboxed)** | Yes (scripts) | No | No | No | No |

---

## Album Art Deep Dive

Terminal album art is the feature where the players diverge most sharply.

| Player | Protocol | Prominence | Quality |
|--------|----------|------------|---------|
| **must** | Kitty, iTerm2, Sixel, halfblocks (via go-termimg) | **Full-width primary panel** — always visible in default layout | Multi-protocol, local-first + online fallback (TheAudioDB) |
| **rmpc** | Kitty, Sixel, iTerm2, Ueberzug++ | Side panel in browser | Good, but secondary to the browser view |
| **kew** | Sixel via Chafa, ASCII fallback | Side cover + full toggle `b` | Excellent Sixel support, supports embedded + folder art |
| **cliamp** | None | Metadata headers only (artist/album text) | No image rendering |
| **cmus** | None | None | Not supported |
| **ncmpcpp** | None | None | Not supported (notify-send scripts only) |

**Observation:** must and kew are the only players that treat album art as a
primary UI element rather than an optional side panel. must gives it the most
screen real estate in its default layout. kew offers the broadest terminal
protocol support via Chafa (which also powers Kitty, iTerm2, and other
protocols transparently).

---

## Eye Candy

Terminal music players span a wide range of visual polish — from purely
utilitarian ncurses layouts to deliberately crafted interfaces. This matters
most to users who spend significant time in the player and care about the
aesthetic experience.

| Dimension | must | cliamp | kew | rmpc | cmus | ncmpcpp |
|-----------|------|--------|-----|------|------|---------|
| **Album art display** | Full-width, prominent, multi-protocol | None | Sixel side/toggle | Side panel, multi-protocol | None | None |
| **Visualizers** | 9 built-in types, real audio capture, fullscreen | Built-in + Lua plugin visualizers | Spectrum + external Chroma | Via Cava (external) | None | Via FIFO + FFTW (config-heavy) |
| **Theme depth** | 6 themes + native Omarchy live-reload | 20 themes + community Omarchy hook | 26+ themes, album-color extraction | No theme system | Terminal colors only | Configurable ncurses colors |
| **Layout variety** | 4 distinct layouts (large to narrow), responsive | Compact mode toggle | Custom pane positioning (4.1+) | Configurable columns | Fixed single layout | Extended song format only |
| **Progress indicators** | **Gradient progress bars with elapsed/total** | Simple percentage | Bar-style progress | Basic indicator | ASCII progress | Text percentage |
| **Typography & icons** | Nerd Font throughout, organized footer icons | Icons in EQ/transport, Winamp-style | Unicode symbols | Icon set | ASCII only | Unicode symbols |
| **Startup presentation** | Bubble Tea logo with album art fallback | Winamp-style header | Album art on launch | Plain text | Plain text | Plain text |
| **Overall vibe** | Polished modern TUI — feels like a GUI app | Retro Winamp — dense, nostalgic | Clean modern — utilitarian with art | Functional — browser-focused | Utilitarian — speed over looks | Utilitarian — feature over form |

must and kew lead the pack here, but in different directions. kew's visual
appeal comes from its excellent Sixel album art and album-color theming.
must's comes from deliberate UI design — gradient progress bars, thoughtful
spacing, color-harmonized themes, smooth view transitions, and a layout that
adapts gracefully to any terminal size. The Bubble Tea framework provides
polish (rounded corners, lipgloss styling) that ncurses-based players simply
can't match.

cliamp's visual identity is distinct: the Winamp-inspired EQ bars, spectrum
analyzer, and compact layout evoke a specific nostalgic feeling. It's not
*polished* in the modern sense, but it's deliberately styled and recognizable.

For Omarchy users specifically, the combination of must's album art, gradient
progress bars, visualizers, and live theme sync creates a cohesive desktop
experience that no other terminal player matches.

---

## Omarchy Integration Comparison

Omarchy users will care about this specifically.

| Dimension | must | cliamp | All others |
|-----------|------|--------|------------|
| **Theme auto-detect** | **Native** — reads Omarchy's current `colors.toml` as fallback in theme chain | Community hook script regenerates cliamp theme on Omarchy switch | None |
| **Theme live-reload** | **Yes** — watches file every 1s, hot-swaps colors | Via hook (theme file is rewritten, cliamp must reload) | None |
| **Desktop widget** | None planned | **Official Quickshell now-playing card** with spectrum analyzer | None |
| **Screenshots** | All feature Omarchy Vurple theme | Screenshots feature various themes | None |

**Bottom line:** cliamp has the deeper integration today (Quickshell widget is
a major differentiator). must has deeper *automatic* theme integration (native
watcher vs. community hook). Neither player locks you into Omarchy — both work
fine on any Linux desktop.

For users who want their music player colors to follow their desktop theme
without configuration, must's native watcher is the simplest path. For users
who want a desktop widget showing what's playing, cliamp's Quickshell card is
unique.

---

## Exclusive Features

Several features in this comparison are exclusive to must — no other player
offers them in any form.

| Exclusive feature | must | Anyone else? |
|-------------------|------|-------------|
| Local + Subsonic hybrid (same playlist) | Yes | No |
| Artist bios, discographies, image galleries | Yes | No |
| Omarchy theme live-reload (native watcher) | Yes | cliamp: community hook |
| Lidarr integration | Yes | No |
| Temp/download directory browser (staging area) | Yes | No |
| Last.fm + ListenBrainz scrobbling (both at once) | Yes | ncmpcpp: Last.fm only, cliamp: via plugin |
| ReplayGain (album + track modes) | Yes | cliamp: none |

---

## Keybinding Philosophy

How each player approaches keyboard control is a major usability dimension.

### must
Keybindings are **always visible in a footer**. Every key is shown on screen
at all times, organized by category (playback, playlist, views, system). No
memorization required. The trade-off: the footer takes screen space, and
bindings are not user-remappable.

### cliamp
**Comprehensive but not discoverable.** 100+ keybindings organized by
category (transport: Space/s/>/<, providers: N/S/P/J/Y/C/M/L/R, EQ: e,
theme: t, visualizer: v, etc.). Press `?` or `Ctrl+K` for the full reference.
Bindings are not user-remappable. Better suited for users who invest time in
learning the reference.

### cmus
**Vi-inspired, fully remappable.** Navigation with `j/k`, commands with `:`
and tab completion, modes (library/tree/playlist/filters). You can rebind
any key to any command. The most idiomatic implementation of vi philosophy
in a music player. Takes time to learn but is extremely efficient once
mastered.

### ncmpcpp
**Vi-style, fully remappable.** F1 shows full keybinding list. Copy the
default bindings file to `~/.config/ncmpcpp/bindings` and edit. Modally
organized (library/playlist/browser/search/tag editor). Similar learning
curve to cmus.

### rmpc
**Vim-like, remappable via TOML config.** Ranger/lf-style browsing with
`j/k`, `/` search. Familiar to users of file managers like lf and ranger.

### kew
**Single-letter commands + F-keys.** `s` shuffle, `r` repeat, `v` visualizer,
`i` cycle colors, `t` themes, `b` toggle album art, `m` lyrics, `.` favorites.
F2-F6 switch views. Fully remappable via `kewrc`. Lower learning curve than
cmus but not as discoverable as must's footer.

### spotify-tui
**Configurable in config.yml.** Defaults: Space (play/pause), `j/k` (navigate),
`/` (search), `?` (help). Spotify-like navigation (left sidebar, main panel).
Easy to pick up for Spotify users.

---

## User-Friendliness Spectrum

The players in this comparison span a wide range of accessibility. Heavily
vi-inspired or modal players reward invested power users but present a steep
initial learning curve. Others prioritize immediate usability.

```
most accessible ────────────────────────────────────────────── most technical
      must ─── kew ─── cliamp ─── rmpc ─── ncmpcpp ─── cmus
```

**must** leads in accessibility: keybindings are always visible in a footer,
the default layout is sensible out of the box, and the four layout modes
adapt to different terminal sizes without manual configuration. Everything
works without reading a manual.

**kew** is next: single-letter commands and F-key views are learnable, and
`kew <keyword>` is intuitive. But some concepts (album-color theming, custom
layout files) require reading.

**cliamp** is powerful but dense: 100+ keybindings across providers, EQ,
visualizers, and themes. The reference card (`?` or `Ctrl+K`) is excellent
but you'll need it. Better for users willing to invest time.

**rmpc** assumes familiarity with both MPD and ranger/lf-style navigation.
Not difficult once you know the conventions, but opaque to newcomers.

**ncmpcpp** and **cmus** are the most programmer-oriented: vi modal
navigation, `:command` mode with tab completion, fully remappable bindings,
and configuration-heavy setups. Extremely efficient once learned, but the
initial friction is real. cmus's live filtering (type to filter your library)
is the one concession to discoverability.

For non-programmers or users who just want to play music without learning
a new paradigm, the gradient is clear: start with must or kew, graduate to
cliamp or rmpc if you need more, invest in cmus or ncmpcpp if you want
maximum power and are comfortable with a vi-like workflow.

---

## Recommendation Matrix

### Choose must if…

- **You want prominent album art** in the terminal — must gives it the most
  space, with multiple protocol support and smart fallbacks.
- **You use Navidrome, Jellyfin, or another Subsonic server** — must is the
  only player that natively mixes local files and Subsonic streams in one
  playlist. Useable local-only, Subsonic-only, or both.
- **You value discoverable keybindings** — the always-visible footer means
  zero memorization. Handy for occasional use or for users who dislike
  reference cards.
- **You want deep but effortless Omarchy theme integration** — must watches
  and reloads your desktop theme automatically. No hooks to install, no
  scripts to run.
- **You want artist info** — bios, discographies, and image galleries in the
  TUI. No other player does this.
- **You want a staging area for new music** — the temp directory browser lets
  you play tracks from download folders before committing them to your main
  library. Unique to must.
- **You want integrated Lidarr monitoring** — see which artists are monitored,
  and open Lidarr from within the TUI.
- **You want ReplayGain** — album and track modes, with instant switching.

### Choose cliamp if…

- **You want the most streaming providers** — Spotify, YouTube, SoundCloud,
  Plex, Jellyfin, Navidrome, Bandcamp, NetEase, radio (30k+), podcasts. No
  other player comes close.
- **You want a parametric EQ** — the 10-band EQ with presets is unique among
  terminal players.
- **You want a plugin system** — Lua sandbox for hook scripts and custom
  visualizers.
- **You want an Omarchy Quickshell widget** — the official now-playing card
  with live spectrum analyzer is unique to cliamp.
- **You want headless/daemon mode** — run cliamp in the background and control
  it via IPC or MPRIS.
- **You want Winamp nostalgia** — the visual style, spectrum analyzer, and EQ
  are deliberately reminiscent of Winamp 2.

### Choose cmus if…

- **You want the simplest setup** — one binary, no server, no config, no
  dependencies. Just `cmus ~/Music`.
- **You have a huge library** — cmus is the fastest player with the largest
  tested libraries. Instantly usable.
- **You want battle-tested reliability** — 20+ years of development. Used in
  production by thousands.
- **You want vi-style control** — fully remappable, `:command` mode, tab
  completion. The most powerful keybinding system of any player here.

### Choose ncmpcpp + MPD if…

- **You want maximum flexibility** — MPD is the most versatile audio backend.
  Swap clients anytime (ncmpcpp, rmpc, Cantata, etc.), run headless, control
  over the network.
- **You want a tag editor** — ncmpcpp's batch tag editor has no equivalent in
  the other players here.
- **You use a multi-room or headless setup** — MPD is designed for this. Run
  on a Raspberry Pi, control from any machine.
- **You want the largest ecosystem** — MPD has dozens of clients, plugins,
  bridges, and integrations built over 20+ years.

### Choose rmpc if…

- **You want a modern MPD client with album art** — rmpc has the best terminal
  image support of any MPD client (Kitty, Sixel, iTerm2, Ueberzug++).
- **You want Cava visualization** — integrates the Cava audio visualizer.
- **You want LRC lyrics** — synced lyrics display alongside album art.
- **You like ranger/lf-style file managers** — the three-column browser will
  feel natural.

### Choose kew if…

- **You think in terms of "what do I want to hear?" not "where is the file?"** —
  `kew radiohead` just works. The CLI-first model is genuinely different from
  every other player here.
- **You want excellent Sixel album art** — kew's Chafa-based rendering is
  arguably the best Sixel implementation in any terminal player.
- **You want album-color-derived theming** — kew can extract 4 colors from the
  current album cover and restyle itself in real time.
- **You care deeply about offline/privacy** — kew is completely offline by
  design, with a formal privacy policy. No streaming, no tracking.
- **You want custom layouts** — kew 4.1 allows positioning each UI pane
  (cover, playlist, library, etc.) arbitrarily via `.layout` files.

### Choose spotify-tui + spotifyd if…

- **Spotify is your primary music source** — this is the best terminal
  experience for Spotify users.
- **You want Spotify Connect** — control playback from your phone, another
  computer, or the TUI. Seamless device switching.
- **You don't have a local music library** — if all your music is on Spotify,
  there's no reason to use the other players.

---

## Verdict

**cliamp** is the most *capable* terminal music player by objective measures:
more providers, an EQ, a plugin system, a desktop widget. If you want one
player that does everything, cliamp is the answer.

But "does everything" is not everyone's goal. **must** makes different trade-offs:
it does fewer things but does them with more polish in the areas that matter
to a particular kind of user — album art as a first-class citizen, keybindings
you never have to memorize, a local+Subsonic hybrid model, artist info views,
a temp directory staging area, and effortless Omarchy theme integration. It
trades breadth for focus, and in doing so picks up several features no other
player offers.

**kew** occupies a similar focused position but from a different angle: its
CLI-first interaction model and excellent Sixel album art make it the best
choice for users who want to type what they want and go.

**cmus** remains the default recommendation for most users: it's fast, stable,
simple, and has the largest install base by far. Its vi-style power is
unmatched once learned, but the learning curve is real.

The other players excel in their niches — ncmpcpp+MPD for modularity, rmpc for
a modern MPD client with art, spotify-tui for Spotify natives. None of them
are wrong choices. It depends on where you want to compromise.

If you're an Omarchy user specifically: cliamp gives you a desktop widget and
broad streaming support. must gives you automatic theme sync and a cleaner
in-terminal experience. Neither is a bad choice — they just prioritize
differently.

---

*This document was written for the must project but strives to be fair to all
players. Pull requests with corrections or updates are welcome.*
